question: Merge vs rebase.
answer:
Merge combines branches with a merge commit — preserves the true history, non-destructive.

Rebase replays your commits on top of another branch — a linear, clean history, but rewrites commits. Never rebase branches others have pulled.
tip: Golden rule: don't rebase shared/public branches.
code: git rebase main   # replay feature commits onto main

question: git fetch vs git pull.
answer: fetch downloads remote changes but doesn't touch your working branch — you can inspect first. pull = fetch + merge (or rebase) into your current branch in one step.
tip: Use fetch + review before merging when you want to see what changed.

question: reset vs revert vs checkout.
answer:
• revert — creates a new commit that undoes a previous one; safe on shared branches.
  • reset — moves the branch pointer (soft/mixed/hard); rewrites history, use locally.
  • checkout / switch / restore — move between branches or restore files.
tip: On a shared branch, undo with revert (safe), not reset (rewrites history).
code:
git revert <hash>   # safe undo
git reset --hard HEAD~1  # local-only

question: What does git stash do?
answer: Temporarily shelves uncommitted changes so you can switch context (e.g. fix an urgent bug on another branch), then reapply them with git stash pop.
tip: git stash pop applies and drops; git stash apply keeps the stash.
code:
git stash
git switch main
# ... later ...
git switch feature && git stash pop

question: What is git cherry-pick?
answer: Applies a single specific commit from one branch onto another — useful for hotfixes you need on multiple branches without merging everything.
tip: Great for porting one bug-fix commit to a release branch.

question: Describe a common branching strategy.
answer: GitFlow — long-lived main and develop, plus feature/*, release/*, and hotfix/* branches. Simpler alternative: trunk-based / GitHub Flow — short-lived feature branches merged to main via pull requests.
tip: Most modern teams favour short-lived branches + PRs over full GitFlow.

question: How do you resolve a merge conflict?
answer: Git marks conflicting regions with <<<<<<< / ======= / >>>>>>>. Edit to the desired result, remove the markers, git add the files, then commit (or git rebase --continue).
tip: Pull/rebase frequently to keep conflicts small and manageable.

question: How do you recover a 'lost' commit?
answer: git reflog logs where HEAD has been, including commits orphaned by a reset/rebase. Find the hash and git checkout or git reset back to it.
tip: reflog is the safety net after a bad reset — commits aren't truly gone immediately.

question: What is squashing and why do it?
answer: Combining several commits into one (via interactive rebase or a squash-merge) to keep the main branch history clean — one logical change = one commit.
tip: Squash-merge on PRs keeps main's history readable without losing branch detail.
code: git rebase -i HEAD~3   # mark commits as 'squash'

question: What is HEAD and a detached HEAD?
answer: HEAD points to your current commit/branch. A detached HEAD means you've checked out a specific commit rather than a branch — new commits there aren't on any branch and can be lost unless you create one.
tip: If you commit in detached HEAD, make a branch before switching away.
code: git switch -c new-branch  # save detached-HEAD work
